context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill one person to save five. I also don’t really get what act consequentialism is supposed to say about the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, and it all kind of blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s un-blur it. Break it into 3 parts:
1. What does act consequentialism evaluate?
2. What would it say in a case where 1 dies and 5 live?
3. Do you think that verdict fits all the cases?

Start with part 1: in act consequentialism, is an act judged by the kind of act it is, or by the total consequences of that particular act?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief, stay within the assignment, and use guided Socratic questions rather than explaining the whole answer. Since the student is confused about multiple related ideas at once, I should first separate the task into smaller parts: the general principle, what act consequentialism says, and then how to apply it to the cases. I will not provide a direct solution or wording for their paper. Instead, I will ask targeted questions that move toward the core distinction: does act consequentialism judge acts by consequences alone, and if so, what would that imply in cases where killing one saves five?

exchanges.student: I think it’s by the total consequences of that particular act, but I’m not confident because then it feels like it would just say “save the five” in basically every version and I’m not seeing why the cases are separated. I’m kind of mixing up the theory with the examples.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your first sentence is the key idea. Don’t overcomplicate it.

If a theory looks only at the outcome of each act, then what verdict would it tend to give whenever killing 1 is the means to saving 5?

Then ask the next question: if that same verdict comes out in Fuel, Blood, and trolley variants, why might philosophers still separate the cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central feature of act consequentialism but is confused about applying it across cases. I should affirm the correct core idea, then use a question that forces comparison between the theory's simple decision rule and the lecturer's multiple cases. This keeps the student doing the reasoning and helps them notice that the separation of cases may matter for evaluating whether act consequentialism gets them right, not for act consequentialism's own verdict.

exchanges.student: I guess it would tend to say killing 1 is okay if that particular act leads to the best overall outcome, like 1 dies instead of 5. But then I get stuck on why Fuel and Blood and the trolley cases aren’t just all the same under that view, because I feel like I’m supposed to notice some moral difference and I’m not sure what difference even matters.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly: under that view, they may collapse together. That’s probably the pressure point of the assignment.

Now shift questions: what differences might someone think matter even if the body count is the same?

Pick 1 or 2: using someone as a means, intending harm, directness of agency, violating a right, killing vs letting die. Which of those seems most important to you in separating the cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the act consequentialist core. The next step is to help them distinguish between what act consequentialism says and what the student may think morally matters. I should prompt them to identify candidate morally relevant differences across cases—intention, means, directness, rights, doing vs allowing—without listing a full theory or giving conclusions. This keeps the answer bite-sized and advances toward the assignment's final evaluative part about whether act consequentialism gets the cases right.

exchanges.student: Maybe using someone as a means and also the difference between killing and letting die? I’m not sure though, because I can kind of talk myself out of each one, and then I end up back at “it’s just numbers,” which feels too simple but also maybe that’s the point?
exchanges.tutor:
Stop trying to solve all distinctions at once. Test just one.

Take “using someone as a means.” In which case does the one person seem used to produce the saving of the five, and in which case does the person’s death seem more like a side effect?

Name two cases and compare them. That will tell you whether “just numbers” misses something.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same issue and expressing uncertainty. I should narrow the scope, ask them to test one distinction on concrete cases, and remind them of the bigger picture: first identify act consequentialism’s verdict, then assess it. I will avoid giving conclusions and push them to generate a comparison themselves.

exchanges.student: I think in the fat man trolley type case the one person is being used as the means, because his body is what stops the trolley, and that feels worse. But in a switch case it seems more like the one dies as a side effect of redirecting the trolley, and I’m still confused why that difference should matter morally instead of just emotionally.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a real philosophical issue, not just confusion.

So ask yourself: what principle would explain why using a person as a tool is morally harder to justify than harming them as a foreseen side effect?

Try stating it in your own rough terms. Don’t aim for perfection—just one sentence. What would that principle protect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made the key distinction and now questions its moral significance. I should push them to articulate a principle that would make the distinction morally relevant, without naming or supplying the doctrine for them. This moves toward the assignment's request for a 'right general account' while maintaining Socratic guidance and not giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: Maybe the principle is that I’m not supposed to treat a person like an object or a resource just because doing that helps more people. I think it would protect something like a person’s dignity or rights, but I’m not sure how to say that without sounding vague.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction. Vagueness is okay at this stage—testing matters more.

Now use your own idea on the cases:
- Which cases would act consequentialism permit?
- Which cases would your dignity/rights idea resist?

Where do those two views give different verdicts? That contrast is the center of your paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a promising candidate principle. I should affirm that direction and now connect it back to the assignment structure: act consequentialism, application to cases, and critique. I must avoid polishing their language, so I will ask them to test their principle against the named cases and identify where act consequentialism and their principle diverge.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism would probably permit basically any case where killing 1 really does save 5, so Fuel, Blood, and the trolley variants might all come out permissible if the consequences are best overall. My dignity/rights idea would resist at least the cases where the 1 is used as a means, but I’m honestly still fuzzy on where Fuel and Blood each fall, so I can’t tell if I’m classifying them right.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t guess the labels—reconstruct the structure of each case.

For Fuel and Blood, ask of each:
1. What exactly is done to the one?
2. Is that harm the means of saving the five, or a side effect?
3. Is the agent killing, or withholding/redirecting aid?

Give me your best one-line description of Fuel first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the broad structure but lacks confidence about specific cases. I should not supply the case classifications directly. Instead, I should ask for the operative feature of each case, because once they identify whether the one person's harm is means or side effect, or killing versus letting die, they can classify it themselves. This remains bite-sized and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on Fuel specifically and I think I’m mushing it together with the others. I remember the general issue, but I can’t cleanly picture what’s done to the one person in that case.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t fake the case from memory. Check your lecture notes/slides for Fuel’s setup first—that detail matters.

While you do that, use the bigger picture: for any case, sort it by means vs side effect and killing vs letting die.

When you find Fuel, give me a 1–2 line summary of what the agent does, and we’ll classify it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on recalling a case detail, so I should avoid pretending certainty about the lecture-specific setup. I will keep the response brief, acknowledge the memory issue, and guide them to recover the case from their notes or by summarizing what they do remember. I must not provide a direct solution or invent the case. I will steer them back to the assignment structure and ask for a minimal recall attempt so we can proceed.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll check because I really don’t trust my memory here. I’m still worried that even with the categories, I won’t know which one is supposed to matter more if a case seems mixed.
exchanges.tutor:
You do not need to know in advance which category matters most. That’s what your argument is for.

Try this method: which distinction best explains your judgments across the most cases with the fewest exceptions?

When you return with Fuel, we’ll test whether “means vs side effect” or “killing vs letting die” does more work.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is anticipating confusion about competing distinctions. I should reduce overwhelm by telling them they do not need a perfect master principle immediately; instead, they should compare candidate distinctions against the cases and see which does better. This is guided discovery toward the final evaluative task. I will keep it concise and ask a forward-moving question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s the part that scrambles me — I can usually have a reaction to each case, but I don’t know how to turn that into one general rule without it breaking somewhere. I’ll look up Fuel and then I guess I need help seeing which distinction actually fits the pattern best.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the real job of the assignment: not just reactions, but a rule that explains them.

So do this next: make a tiny grid for Fuel, Blood, switch, fat man—just your verdict on each.
Then ask: which single distinction explains that pattern best?

Bring me your Fuel summary plus your verdicts, and we’ll test the rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing a common difficulty: moving from case reactions to a defensible general principle. I should normalize that, keep boundaries, and avoid giving a rule. The best next step is to have them articulate their verdicts case-by-case and then test candidate distinctions for explanatory power. This follows Socratic method, guided discovery, and keeps the response bite-sized.
